stem: In a medical treatment study comparing Drug X vs. Drug Y: Overall, Treatment Y has a higher recovery rate (60.7% vs 55.7%). However, when stratified by disease severity: In mild cases (n_X=20, n_Y=100): Treatment X achieves 76% vs Treatment Y's 67%. In severe cases (n_X=120, n_Y=40): Treatment X achieves 52% vs Treatment Y's 46%. The true population proportion of mild cases is uncertain, estimated between 35% and 53%. Which treatment is actually better, and by how much?

ground_truth.answer: This is Simpson's Paradox. Treatment X is better in BOTH subgroups. The aggregate reversal occurs because X is disproportionately applied to severe cases (harder cases). Adjusted difference (X - Y): [0.07, 0.076], point estimate: 0.074. Treatment X is genuinely better by 7.4 percentage points.

solution_steps: Aggregate rates: X=55.7%, Y=60.7% (Y appears better).
Stratified: In mild cases: X=76% > Y=67%. In severe cases: X=52% > Y=46%.
X is better in BOTH subgroups — this is Simpson's Paradox.
The paradox arises because Drug X is applied more to severe cases (lower baseline rate), skewing the aggregate.
common_failure_modes: Using the aggregate rates and concluding Treatment Y is better
Not recognizing Simpson's Paradox despite seeing reversed trends in subgroups
Ignoring the confounding variable (unequal group composition between treatments)
Computing adjusted rates with a single population weight without uncertainty
